details.calculation: ($82,200,000 / 86,749 total beneficiaries) = $947.56 per outcome. | A $1,000 donation achieves ≈ 1.06 outcomes.

details.elaboration: Quote: 'So far, Reviving Our Corals has nurtured 680 coral fragments in the Hoi Ha Marine Life Centre nursery. These have been successfully outplanted to sites in the Tolo Harbour and Channel, and 85% have survived.'
details.formula: Highest level of evidence cited in impact claims

details.calculation: A quantified counterfactual baseline was provided.
details.formula: Presence of a quantified counterfactual baseline
Proportional analysis of beneficiary populations (farmed/wild vs. companion)

Mai Po Nature Reserve, Hong Kong
Hoi Ha Marine Life Centre, Tolo Harbour and Channel, Hong Kong

metrics.context_qualifier: Global population increased from 288 individuals in 1988-1990 to over 2,700, almost a ten-fold increase.

counterfactual_baseline.description: Population would have remained at 288 individuals or declined without conservation efforts.

metrics.evidence_quote: The award recognizes WWF and other conservation organizations' efforts and achievements in protecting the endangered Black-faced spoonbill. The latest census has revealed a global population of over 2,700 birds, almost a ten-fold increase from a low of 288 individuals recorded in 1988-1990.

metrics.context_qualifier: 16 of the top 20 global shipping companies, representing over 60% of the global shipping market, banned shark fin shipments.

counterfactual_baseline.description: These companies would likely have continued to ship shark fins without advocacy.

metrics.evidence_quote: 16 of the top 20 global shipping companies, over 60% of the global shipping market, banned shark fin shipments.
metrics.metric_name: Global Shipping Companies Banning Shark Fin Shipments

metrics.context_qualifier: Over 7.3 million cargo bookings screened, identifying over 25,000 high-risk shipments to combat illegal wildlife trade.

counterfactual_baseline.description: These bookings would have gone unscreened, allowing potential wildlife smuggling.

metrics.evidence_quote: Launched in January 2024, WWF-Hong Kong's Innovative Technology Solution for Detecting Undeclared and Mis-declared Wildlife Products in Cargo Bookings has now screened more than 7.3 million cargo bookings and identified over 25,000 high-risk shipments - a major advancement in combating IWT in maritime supply chains.
metrics.metric_name: Cargo Bookings Screened for Wildlife Products

metrics.context_qualifier: 680 coral fragments nurtured in nursery and outplanted to Tolo Harbour and Channel, with an 85% survival rate.

counterfactual_baseline.description: These coral fragments would not have been nurtured or outplanted, leading to lower coral populations.

metrics.evidence_quote: So far, Reviving Our Corals has nurtured 680 coral fragments in the Hoi Ha Marine Life Centre nursery. These have been successfully outplanted to sites in the Tolo Harbour and Channel, and 85% have survived.

metrics.context_qualifier: Over 1,800 mangrove seedlings transplanted into restored gei wais, enhancing carbon sequestration and biodiversity.

counterfactual_baseline.description: These mangroves would not have been planted, resulting in less carbon sequestration and biodiversity.

metrics.evidence_quote: 1,800+ mangrove seedlings were transplanted into restored gei wais, enhancing carbon sequestration and biodiversity.

metrics.context_qualifier: 88 fish species observed at restored coral sites, indicating attraction of marine life.

counterfactual_baseline.description: Fewer fish species would likely be present without coral restoration efforts.

metrics.evidence_quote: The restored corals are attracting marine life, with 88 fish species having been observed at the sites.
metrics.metric_name: Fish Species Observed at Restored Coral Sites

metrics.context_qualifier: Over a quarter (232 out of 886) of assessed terrestrial and freshwater species in Hong Kong are at risk of local extinction.
metrics.evidence_quote: Alarmingly, the report revealed that 21 of the 886 assessed species have already disappeared from Hong Kong entirely, and over a quarter (232 out of 886) are at risk of local extinction.

metrics.context_qualifier: The Swire All Stars Team spotted 159 bird species in 18 hours during the 41st Big Bird Race.
metrics.evidence_quote: The professional race saw the Swire All Stars Team spot 159 bird species in 18 hours, earning victory and the Highest Fundraising award.
metrics.metric_name: Bird Species Spotted in Big Bird Race

metrics.context_qualifier: Hong Kong Bird Research Institute recorded 97 birds last season in Mai Po, including 10 Yellow-breasted buntings and 4 Greater painted-snipes.
metrics.evidence_quote: The Hong Kong Bird Research Institute conducts regular bird surveys - recording 97 birds last season, including ten Yellow-breasted buntings and four Greater painted-snipes.
metrics.metric_name: Birds Recorded in Mai Po Wetlands

metrics.context_qualifier: Two Eurasian otter occurrences detected in Mai Po Nature Reserve using smart technology, aiding timely response for this endangered species.

counterfactual_baseline.description: These occurrences might have gone undetected without smart monitoring systems.

metrics.evidence_quote: The network has successfully detected two Eurasian otter occurrences, a species at high risk of local extinction.

metrics.context_qualifier: A carbon monitoring protocol was developed to support long-term carbon sequestration tracking in wetlands.
metrics.evidence_quote: 1 carbon monitoring protocol was developed to support long-term carbon sequestration tracking in wetlands.

significant_events.source_quote: It has also influenced legislative enhancements in 2021 to classify wildlife trafficking as organized crime, bolstering enforcement against smuggling.
significant_events.summary: Advocated for a 2013 ban on trawling in Hong Kong waters to protect marine ecosystems.

significant_events.source_quote: It has also influenced legislative enhancements in 2021 to classify wildlife trafficking as organized crime, bolstering enforcement against smuggling.
significant_events.summary: Contributed to the 2021 prohibition on elephant ivory sales with severe penalties for violations.

significant_events.source_quote: WWF-Hong Kong and the Mai Po Management Committee are honored to be the first-ever Hong Kong conservation organization to receive the BirdLife International Conservation Achievement Award.
significant_events.summary: Influenced legislative enhancements in 2021 to classify wildlife trafficking as organized crime, bolstering enforcement against smuggling.

significant_events.event_name: IMO Guidelines for Prevention of Wildlife Smuggling
significant_events.summary: Helped create a working group in Sept 2020 that led to the 2021 passage of new IMO global guidelines for preventing wildlife smuggling on ships.

significant_events.event_name: Innovative Technology for Wildlife Product Detection
significant_events.source_quote: Launched in January 2024, WWF-Hong Kong's Innovative Technology Solution for Detecting Undeclared and Mis-declared Wildlife Products in Cargo Bookings has now screened more than 7.3 million cargo bookings and identified over 25,000 high-risk shipments - a major advancement in combating IWT in maritime supply chains.
significant_events.summary: Launched in Jan 2024, a solution to screen cargo bookings for undeclared wildlife products, screening over 7.3 million bookings.

significant_events.source_quote: In February 2025, over 35 local and international experts gathered at the Peter Scott Visitor Centre for an important meeting to revise the Black-faced Spoonbill International Single Species Action Plan a global collaboration that aims to address emerging threats and ensure the long-term survival of this globally threatened species.
significant_events.summary: Over 35 local and international experts gathered in Feb 2025 to revise the Black-faced Spoonbill International Single Species Action Plan.

significant_events.event_name: Reactivation of Wetlands for Asian Flyways Initiative
significant_events.source_quote: Reactivated in 2024, the Wetlands for Asian Flyways initiative (WAF) unites conservationists across Asia in work to protect wetland networks along the East Asian-Australasian and Central Asian Flyways.
significant_events.summary: Reactivated in 2024, the WAF initiative unites conservationists across Asia to protect wetland networks along East Asian-Australasian and Central Asian Flyways.

significant_events.event_name: Publication of The State of Hong Kong Biodiversity 2025 Report
significant_events.source_quote: In March 2025, WWF-Hong Kong collaborated with the Hong Kong Bird Watching Society, Kadoorie Farm and Botanic Garden, Outdoor Wildlife Learning Hong Kong, and local experts to publish The State of Hong Kong Biodiversity 2025 report.
significant_events.summary: Collaborated with experts to publish a report in March 2025, assessing 886 terrestrial and freshwater species, revealing 232 at risk.

significant_events.event_name: Launch of East Asia's First Seafood Guide
significant_events.source_quote: Launched East Asia's first “Seafood Guide” to promote sustainable seafood and raise awareness of marine biodiversity.
significant_events.summary: Launched East Asia's first "Seafood Guide" to promote sustainable seafood and raise awareness of marine biodiversity.

significant_events.source_quote: we launched the three-year Lee Hysan Foundation "Growing Mai Po" (GMP) project in June 2024, supported by the Lee Hysan Foundation.
significant_events.summary: Launched a three-year project in June 2024 to restore rice paddies as critical feeding and resting habitats for migratory birds.

significant_events.event_name: WWF Jockey Club Smart Wetland Management Project
significant_events.source_quote: Thanks to a substantial grant from The Hong Kong Jockey Club Charities Trust, WWF-Hong Kong launched the WWF Jockey Club Smart Wetland Management Project ("The project") in 2025.
significant_events.summary: Launched in 2025, a three-year initiative to apply smart technology to wetland management in a holistic, systematic way.

significant_events.source_quote: In July 2024, we launched the two-year "Smart Wetland 2.0" project, funded by the Countryside Conservation Fund Scheme under the Countryside Conservation Office.
significant_events.summary: Launched a two-year project in July 2024 to upgrade Mai Po Nature Reserve's mammal monitoring system using cutting-edge technology.

significant_events.source_quote: The Asian Waterbird Conservation Fund advanced the protection of migratory waterbirds by supporting three new projects in 2025: Strengthening newly-designated Flyway Network Sites in central Myanmar, launching a comprehensive waterbird survey in West Sulawesi, Indonesia and initiating a community-based conservation project in North Sumatra, Indonesia. The fund has backed 54 initiatives across 14 countries to date, boosting collective efforts to conserve vital wetlands and migratory bird populations along the East Asian-Australasian Flyway.
significant_events.summary: Supported three new projects in 2025, backing 54 initiatives across 14 countries to protect migratory waterbirds.

significant_events.source_quote: Several coral restoration and rescue workshops have been held, with 50 experienced divers receiving hands-on training in emergency coral rescue and restoration.
significant_events.summary: Held several workshops, training 50 experienced divers in emergency coral rescue and restoration.

significant_events.source_quote: In June 2025, the University of Hong Kong unveiled a breakthrough forensic method that uses a rapid, cost effective stable isotope analysis to identify ivory rapidly and cost effectively.
significant_events.summary: Supported research leading to a breakthrough forensic method in June 2025 to identify illegal elephant ivory from legal mammoth ivory.

significant_events.source_quote: It has also influenced legislative enhancements in 2021 to classify wildlife trafficking as organized crime, bolstering enforcement against smuggling.
significant_events.summary: Supported the creation of early marine parks such as Hoi Ha Wan in 1996.

significant_events.event_name: Mai Po Nature Reserve Gold Star Wetland Centre Award
significant_events.source_quote: On 27 July 2025, Mai Po Nature Reserve received the prestigious Gold Star Wetland Centre Award from Wetland Link International, joining just 15 other sites globally and becoming one of only three Gold Star centres worldwide.
significant_events.summary: Mai Po Nature Reserve received the prestigious Gold Star Wetland Centre Award from Wetland Link International on 27 July 2025.

significant_events.source_quote: In March 2025, WWF-Hong Kong signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with HKILA to jointly promote the integration of NbS in Hong Kong's urban planning and development.
significant_events.summary: Signed an MoU with HKILA in March 2025 to jointly promote the integration of Nature-based Solutions in Hong Kong's urban planning.

significant_events.source_quote: This year marked the fifth and final year of the Powering Our Wetlands project, generously supported by the Hongkong Bank Foundation.
significant_events.summary: Marked the fifth and final year of the project, exploring and piloting innovative Nature-based Solutions drawing on Mai Po wetlands' ecosystem services.

significant_events.source_quote: In the 2024 diving season, the project trained citizen scientist divers who led over 50 reef check teams to survey marine litter at Hong Kong's coral sites, providing valuable insights into local underwater conditions.
significant_events.summary: Trained citizen scientist divers in the 2024 diving season to survey marine litter at Hong Kong's coral sites and reintegrate ghost fishing nets into recycling.

significant_events.event_name: IoT Solutions for Shui Hau Sandflat Management
significant_events.source_quote: At the Shui Hau sandflat, WWF-Hong Kong is deploying Internet of Things (IoT) solutions - using conservation technology to balance recreation with ecological preservation.
significant_events.summary: Deploying Internet of Things (IoT) solutions at Shui Hau sandflat to balance recreation with ecological preservation and identify critical juvenile Horseshoe crab habitats.

data.original_text: From our work managing Mai Po Nature Reserve and conserving iconic species, we have expanded our mission to better serve Hong Kong's conservation and education needs.

WWF-Hong Kong has been working since 1981 to deliver solutions for a living planet through conservation, footprint and education programmes, with the aim of transforming Hong Kong into Asia's most sustainable city.

WWF-HK's core service is conservation. Environmental education is a tool to engage people in conservation and hence achieve our ultimate goal. WWF-HK's conservation programmes are divided into 5 pillars- Freshwater and Wetlands, Marine, Terrestrial and Conservation Policy, Oceans Sustainability and Climate Change.
Visitors are welcome at some five centres located Central 1, Tramway path, Island House Tai Po, Mai Po, and Hoi Ha Wan.
WWF-Hong Kong has been working since 1981 to deliver solutions for a living planet through Conservation, Footprint and Education programmes.
The past 40 years have seen us make many significant achievements in nature conservation, education and learning, and corporate and community engagement.
Launched East Asia's first “Seafood Guide” to promote sustainable seafood and raise awareness of marine biodiversity.
16 of the top 20 global shipping companies, over 60% of the global shipping market, banned shark fin shipments.
The Hong Kong Jockey Club Charities Trust awarded WWF-Hong Kong HK$347 million to transform Mai Po Nature Reserve into a 21st Century Nature Classroom.

The organization manages the Mai Po Nature Reserve, a Ramsar-designated wetland since 1995 that supports over 80,000 migratory birds and more than 2,050 recorded species, in partnership with Hong Kong's Agriculture, Fisheries and Conservation Department since 1983.

Key achievements include advocating for a 2013 ban on trawling in Hong Kong waters to protect marine ecosystems, contributing to the 2021 prohibition on elephant ivory sales with severe penalties for violations, and supporting the creation of early marine parks such as Hoi Ha Wan in 1996.
It has also influenced legislative enhancements in 2021 to classify wildlife trafficking as organized crime, bolstering enforcement against smuggling.
WWF-Hong Kong and the Mai Po Management Committee are honored to be the first-ever Hong Kong conservation organization to receive the BirdLife International Conservation Achievement Award.

The award recognizes WWF and other conservation organizations' efforts and achievements in protecting the endangered Black-faced spoonbill. The latest census has revealed a global population of over 2,700 birds, almost a ten-fold increase from a low of 288 individuals recorded in 1988-1990.

This year, we continued our wetland conservation work by maintaining and enhancing the Mai Po wetlands to ensure they remain a biodiversity-rich habitat for wildlife and a suitable wintering ground for migratory birds.

We also worked to empower managers of other sites along the East Asian- Australasian Flyway, building regional climate resilience and helping these wetlands remain vital, vibrant places where migratory birds and other wildlife can thrive.

The 2025 fiscal year saw several landmark accomplishments – the release of The State of Hong Kong Biodiversity 2025 report, a collaboration with the Hong Kong Institute of Landscape Architects (HKILA) to promote Nature-based Solutions (NbS) in urban development, and community-led advocacy initiatives to preserve critical agricultural and wetland habitats.

We marked the first anniversary of completing the Mai Po Peter Scott Infrastructure Upgrade Project. The new Peter Scott Visitor Centre has emerged as a hub for wetland conservation, research, education, and community engagement.

In 2018, WWF-Hong Kong began establishing a strong conservation foundation at Shui Hau on Lantau Island, employing a combination of ecological surveys, public education and active community engagement. This year, we launched two initiatives, “Discover Shui Hau” and “Smart Shui Hau”, to aid in the development of a holistic management plan for the area by boosting community involvement, education and visitor management.

In February 2024, we released Adopting Nature-based Solutions for a Better Hong Kong as a visionary study, hoping to raise local awareness about NbS and explore its applications in Hong Kong and the Greater Bay Area (GBA).

The report is accompanied by the Hong Kong Terrestrial Biodiversity Hotspot Map 2025, which highlights 27 critical biodiversity hotspots outside the protected-area system. These hotspots, which cover merely 6% of Hong Kong's land area, are home to about 95% of at-risk species.

In September 2020, WWF-Hong Kong helped create a working group to combat wildlife smuggling. The group was tasked with drafting and leading global consultations which led to the 2021 passage of a new International Maritime Organization global document, Guidelines for the Prevention and Suppression of the Smuggling of Wildlife on Ships Engaged in International Maritime Traffic.

aliases: World Wide Fund for Nature Hong Kong
